title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: Your employer has asked you to write a report on employee satisfaction levels within your department. Your report should summarise the findings of a recent staff survey, identify the main areas of concern, and recommend concrete steps management could take to improve morale and retention.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Employee Satisfaction in the Digital Services Department

Introduction
This report summarises the findings of the annual staff satisfaction survey, completed by all 34 members of the Digital Services Department in February. It highlights the key areas of concern and proposes a set of practical measures to address them.

Survey Findings
Overall satisfaction levels have declined by twelve percentage points compared to the previous year. The three issues cited most frequently were:
- Insufficient recognition of individual contributions (68% of respondents).
- Lack of clarity regarding promotion criteria and career pathways (61%).
- Poor communication from senior management regarding organisational changes (57%).

On a positive note, the vast majority of respondents expressed satisfaction with their colleagues and the quality of collaboration within project teams.

Areas of Concern
The data suggest that disengagement is primarily driven by a perceived lack of visibility and professional opportunity rather than by working conditions. Several team members with more than five years of service indicated they were actively considering alternative employment, which represents a significant retention risk.

Recommendations
The following steps are recommended:
- Introducing a quarterly recognition scheme that publicly acknowledges outstanding contributions.
- Publishing a clear, transparent promotion framework with defined competency criteria at each grade level.
- Holding monthly all-hands briefings to allow senior management to share strategic updates and answer questions directly.

Conclusion
The survey reveals that staff are broadly committed to their work but feel undervalued and underinformed. Addressing these concerns proactively will be essential to preventing further decline in retention and morale.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt: A local newspaper has been running a series on the challenges facing young people today. You have been invited to contribute a letter to the series expressing your views on the most significant challenge young people in your country currently face and what you believe should be done about it.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I write in response to your invitation to contribute to the 'Challenges Facing Young People' series, a discussion that I believe has rarely been more timely.

In my view, the most serious challenge confronting young people in this country today is the profound unaffordability of housing. For the generation now in their twenties and early thirties, the prospect of ever owning a home in the city where they were raised—or indeed in any major urban centre—has become remote to the point of being effectively impossible for most. The gap between stagnant graduate wages and spiralling property prices has widened to an extent that was structurally inconceivable to previous generations. The consequence is not merely financial: the absence of stable, affordable housing undermines mental health, delays family formation, and forces many talented individuals to emigrate in search of economic realities that their home country can no longer provide.

The solutions are neither simple nor painless, but they are known. A sustained programme of social housing construction, a genuine reform of planning laws to unlock development land in high-demand areas, and stronger regulation of the private rental market to protect tenants are all measures that have been recommended repeatedly and implemented insufficiently.

Political will, rather than policy knowledge, is what is lacking. I would encourage your readers to make housing a defining issue at every future election until those in a position to act finally do so.

Yours faithfully,
Daniel Okafor

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: An international language learning website is inviting users to review a television series or podcast that they found genuinely useful or entertaining for improving their English. Describe the content, explain what makes it effective or enjoyable, and say who you would recommend it to. Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Genuinely Educational, Genuinely Entertaining: 'Sherlock' (BBC)

Of the many television programmes I have used to supplement my English studies over the years, the BBC's 'Sherlock' stands out as both the most linguistically rewarding and the most compulsively watchable.

The series is a contemporary reimagining of Arthur Conan Doyle's famous detective, transplanted from Victorian London to the present day. The writing is exceptionally dense, witty, and deliberately fast-paced—a quality that can initially feel intimidating but which pays extraordinary dividends for learners willing to invest in it. The dialogue is rich in idiomatic British English, inferential reasoning, and precisely calibrated irony, providing constant exposure to the kind of natural, high-register language that textbooks rarely capture.

From a purely linguistic standpoint, the programme is a masterclass in how English speakers communicate subtext: what characters mean, as opposed to what they say, is frequently more important than the literal content of dialogue. Learning to read this gap is one of the most valuable and elusive skills in advanced English, and few programmes model it as vividly.

I would recommend 'Sherlock' particularly to intermediate and upper-intermediate learners who find themselves plateauing with conventional study materials and want exposure to natural, challenging language in an engaging context. Subtitles in English are strongly advised for the first viewing of each episode.

The series is available on major streaming platforms. Start with Series One—you will very likely not stop there.
